perturbation.original_text: a. The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: a. The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the three-year confidentiality period introduces a legal contradiction. North Carolina law, while not explicitly mandating a specific duration for confidentiality agreements, implies a reasonable timeframe based on the nature of the information and industry standards. Removing the specified period creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act provides legal recourse for misappropriation of trade secrets. However, the absence of a defined confidentiality period weakens the enforceability of the agreement, potentially conflicting with the implied requirement of a reasonable duration for trade secret protection. By completely omitting a reasonable duration, the agreement undermines the protection intended by the statute, as the 'reasonable duration' will be decided by the courts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the three-year confidentiality period, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that while North Carolina law doesn't specify a duration, a 'reasonable timeframe' is implied. Removing the timeframe weakens enforceability and undermines trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret, emphasizing the need for efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing the confidentiality period, the changed text fails to provide a reasonable duration for maintaining secrecy, creating ambiguity and potentially conflicting with the implied requirement of reasonableness under North Carolina law regarding trade secret protection, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information. Each party represents that such degree of care provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information' weakens the standard of care required. North Carolina law expects a reasonable standard to protect trade secrets. By eliminating this specific standard, the contract becomes ambiguous and creates a legal risk, as reasonable can mean anything.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act requires a reasonable standard of care in protecting trade secrets. By changing from a standard of care equal to that which the parties use to protect their own proprietary information to a reasonable standard of care, the agreement is now ambiguous and it contradicts the purpose of the Act. The parties may now claim that their standard of care is low, which could undermine the entire confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the standard of care required to protect proprietary information, moving from 'at least the same degree of care' to a 'reasonable degree of care.' The Law Explanation states that this change introduces ambiguity that contradicts the purpose of the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act, potentially allowing parties to claim a low standard of care. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'trade secret,' includes the provision that the information is 'the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' While the changed text uses the word 'reasonable,' the original text provided a more specific and enforceable standard, which is now omitted. The omission introduces the risk of a lower standard of care, undermining the protection intended by the Act. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: b. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, as well as the legal relations of the parties arising hereunder, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina.
perturbation.changed_text: b. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, as well as the legal relations of the parties arising hereunder, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the forum where the claim is made.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause from North Carolina to 'the forum where the claim is made' creates a potential contradiction. While parties can generally choose a governing law, doing so can impact the protections afforded under North Carolina law. For instance, if a claim is made in a jurisdiction with weaker trade secret protections, the disclosing party loses the benefit of North Carolina's specific regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The parties could select the laws of another state that have weaker protection standards than those defined in the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act. This change contradicts the purpose of the Act by taking the case out of NC courts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law of North Carolina with 'the forum where the claim is made.' This introduces a contradiction because it potentially removes the protections afforded by North Carolina's specific laws, such as the Trade Secrets Protection Act (G.S. 66-152). The original contract specifies North Carolina law, while the changed text allows for the application of laws from other jurisdictions, which may have weaker protections. This undermines the intent of North Carolina law, as described in the Law Explanation, and directly contradicts the protections provided by the Trade Secrets Protection Act. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this shift could weaken trade secret protections, which is a direct contradiction of the purpose of the Act.

perturbation.original_text: Each receiving party further acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each receiving party acknowledges that damages may be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the acknowledgment that damages would be inadequate. In North Carolina, injunctive relief is a standard remedy for trade secret misappropriation. By suggesting damages might be adequate, the contract weakens the right to seek an injunction, potentially conflicting with legal precedents favoring injunctive relief in trade secret cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation. By changing from 'damages would not be an adequate remedy' to 'damages may be an adequate remedy,' the agreement creates ambiguity and could prevent the disclosing party from seeking an injunction in the event of a breach, conflicting with the statutory remedy afforded by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-153 § 66-153. Action for misappropriation. The owner of a trade secret shall have remedy by civil action for misappropriation of his trade secret. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy, which supports seeking injunctive relief for a breach. The changed text states damages 'may be an adequate remedy.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the ability to seek injunctive relief, potentially conflicting with the statutory remedy afforded by the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act, as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that injunctive relief is a standard remedy in North Carolina trade secret cases. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'The owner of a trade secret shall have remedy by civil action for misappropriation of his trade secret,' supports the availability of remedies, including injunctive relief, for trade secret misappropriation. The change in the contract weakens the ability to obtain such a remedy by suggesting damages could be adequate, directly impacting the rights provided to the owner of a trade secret under North Carolina law. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, as it removes essential information that would normally allow for specific legal recourse.
